snippets.title: Stack with a list
snippets.code:
stack: list[str] = []
stack.append("a")    # push
stack.append("b")
stack.append("c")
print(stack[-1])      # peek -> 'c'
print(stack.pop())    # pop  -> 'c'
print(stack)          # ['a', 'b']
snippets.explanation: append and pop on the end are O(1); the list IS the stack.

snippets.title: Queue with collections.deque
snippets.code:
from collections import deque

q: deque[str] = deque()
q.append("req-A")     # enqueue at back
q.append("req-B")
q.append("req-C")
print(q.popleft())    # dequeue from front -> 'req-A'
print(q)              # deque(['req-B', 'req-C'])
snippets.explanation: deque is doubly-linked under the hood; both ends are O(1).

snippets.title: Wrong: list as a queue
snippets.code:
q = []
for i in range(10):
    q.append(i)
# q.pop(0) is O(n); doing this in a loop is O(n^2):
while q:
    item = q.pop(0)
    process(item)
snippets.explanation: list.pop(0) shifts every later element — use collections.deque instead.

snippets.title: Priority queue with heapq
snippets.code:
import heapq

# Min-heap of (score, item) tuples — smallest score at the top.
pq: list[tuple[float, str]] = []
heapq.heappush(pq, (0.91, "chunk-A"))
heapq.heappush(pq, (0.87, "chunk-B"))
heapq.heappush(pq, (0.95, "chunk-C"))

# Pop highest-priority by negating the score (Python heapq is a min-heap):
scored = [(-s, item) for s, item in pq]
heapq.heapify(scored)
best = heapq.heappop(scored)
print(best)   # (-0.95, 'chunk-C')
snippets.explanation: heapq is a min-heap; for max-heap behavior negate the priority. Top-k uses heapq.nlargest.
